title: Crude oil — where it comes from and how we separate it
examTip: Shorter carbon chains = lower boiling point, more flammable, less viscous, rise higher in fractionating column. Learn the fractions in order.
introTips: Crude oil is a mixture — fractional distillation separates it by boiling point, not by chemical reaction.
Chain length controls EVERYTHING: boiling point, viscosity, flammability, and usefulness.
Cracking is the answer to the supply/demand mismatch — long chains in, short chains out.
intro: Almost everything that burns in an engine, powers an aircraft, or coats a road surface comes from one source: crude oil. It's been underground for millions of years, formed from the compressed remains of ancient sea creatures. On its own it's almost useless — a thick, dark mixture that won't do anything particularly well. The brilliant bit is what we do with it: we heat it, let it rise up a tower, and collect the different hydrocarbons as they condense at different heights. That single process underlies the entire petrochemical industry.

keyPoints.heading: What crude oil is
keyPoints.content: Crude oil is a mixture of hydrocarbons — compounds containing only carbon and hydrogen. It formed over millions of years from the remains of marine organisms compressed under heat and pressure (a fossil fuel). Because it took so long to form, it is non-renewable. Most hydrocarbons in crude oil are alkanes — saturated hydrocarbons with only C-C single bonds and the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. Crude oil itself is not very useful as a mixture — it must be separated into fractions before use. A fraction is a group of hydrocarbons with similar boiling points (and similar chain lengths), collected from the same height in a fractionating column.
writeBullets: Crude oil — a mixture of hydrocarbons (compounds containing C and H only)
Formed from remains of ancient marine organisms over millions of years — non-renewable fossil fuel
Most hydrocarbons in crude oil are alkanes: CₙH₂ₙ₊₂, saturated (C-C single bonds only)
Crude oil must be separated into fractions before it is useful
Fraction — a group of hydrocarbons with similar boiling points, collected together

keyTerms.def: A compound containing only carbon and hydrogen atoms.
A fuel formed over millions of years from the compressed remains of living organisms; non-renewable.
A group of hydrocarbons with similar boiling points collected together during fractional distillation.

keyPoints.memTip: Crude oil = ancient sea life squeezed for millions of years into a useful (but messy) mix of hydrocarbons.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often say crude oil is made of 'oil' — make sure they know it is specifically a mixture of hydrocarbons, mostly alkanes.

keyPoints.heading: Fractional distillation
keyPoints.content: Crude oil is separated by fractional distillation in a tall fractionating column. The crude oil is heated until it vaporises and is fed into the column. The column is hotter at the bottom and cooler at the top. Vapours rise up the column and cool as they go. Each fraction condenses (turns back to liquid) when it reaches the temperature that matches its boiling point. Short-chain hydrocarbons have lower boiling points — they stay as vapour longer and rise higher before condensing, so they are collected near the top. Long-chain hydrocarbons have higher boiling points — they condense lower down, near the bottom. This is a physical process — no chemical bonds are broken or formed. The crude oil is separated by differences in boiling point, not by chemical reaction.
writeBullets: Fractionating column: hotter at the bottom, cooler at the top
Crude oil heated → vapourises → vapours rise up the column
Each fraction condenses at the height where temperature matches its boiling point
Short chains: low boiling point → rise high → collected near top
Long chains: high boiling point → condense low → collected near bottom
Physical separation — no chemical bonds broken or formed

keyTerms.def: A physical process that separates a mixture of liquids with different boiling points by evaporation and condensation in a fractionating column.
A tall tower used in the separation of crude oil; it has a temperature gradient — hotter at the bottom, cooler at the top.

keyPoints.memTip: Hot at the bottom, cold at the top — short molecules float to the top, long ones sink. Like a bubble bath where the big bubbles stay low.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students sometimes describe fractional distillation as a chemical process — emphasise it is physical. No new substances are formed.

keyPoints.heading: Properties of fractions
keyPoints.content: As the chain length increases, the boiling point increases, viscosity increases, and flammability decreases. Fractions from crude oil (shortest to longest chain): Refinery gases (C1-C4): gases at room temperature, very low boiling point, highly flammable — used as LPG (liquefied petroleum gas) for camping stoves and heating. Petrol/gasoline (C5-C10): liquid, low boiling point, very flammable — used as car fuel. Kerosene/paraffin (C10-C16): liquid, medium boiling point — used as jet fuel. Diesel (C16-C25): liquid, higher boiling point, less flammable — used in lorries and trains. Heavy fuel oil: viscous liquid, high boiling point — used in power stations and ships. Bitumen: solid/semi-solid at room temperature, very high boiling point, very viscous — used for road surfacing and roofing.
writeBullets: Shorter chains → lower boiling point, lower viscosity, higher flammability
Refinery gases (C1-C4): gases, very flammable, used as LPG
Petrol (C5-C10): liquid, very flammable, car fuel
Kerosene (C10-C16): liquid, jet fuel
Diesel (C16-C25): liquid, lorries and trains
Bitumen: solid, very viscous and high BP, road surfaces

keyTerms.def: A measure of how easily a liquid flows; high viscosity means it flows slowly (thick). Longer chains = higher viscosity.
The longest-chain fraction from crude oil; solid/semi-solid at room temperature, used for road surfacing.
A fraction of crude oil with carbon chains of approximately C10-C16; used as jet fuel.

keyPoints.memTip: Small = skimpy (low BP, low viscosity, very flammable). Big = bulky (high BP, high viscosity, less flammable). Think of cooking oil (thick, doesn't ignite easily) vs lighter fluid (thin, ignites immediately).
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often get viscosity and boiling point trends the wrong way round. Longer chains = MORE intermolecular forces = higher BP and higher viscosity.

keyPoints.heading: Cracking
keyPoints.content: Cracking breaks down long-chain hydrocarbons into shorter, more useful molecules. This is necessary because demand for short-chain fractions (petrol, kerosene) far exceeds their natural supply in crude oil — there is too much of the long-chain fractions. Two types of cracking: Thermal cracking uses very high temperatures (~1000°C) and high pressure — produces a high proportion of alkenes. Catalytic cracking uses a zeolite catalyst at a lower temperature (~500°C) and lower pressure — more energy efficient and produces mainly branched alkanes and some alkenes. Products of cracking always include: shorter alkanes (useful as fuels) + alkenes (identified by their C=C double bond, used to make polymers and other chemicals). Example: decane → octane + ethene (C₁₀H₂₂ → C₈H₁₈ + C₂H₄).
writeBullets: Cracking — breaking long-chain hydrocarbons into shorter, more useful molecules
Needed because demand for short-chain fractions exceeds supply
Thermal cracking: ~1000°C, high pressure — produces mainly alkenes
Catalytic cracking: zeolite catalyst, ~500°C, lower pressure — more efficient
Products: shorter alkanes (fuels) + alkenes (for making polymers)
Alkenes identified by C=C double bond; example: C₁₀H₂₂ → C₈H₁₈ + C₂H₄

keyTerms.def: A process that breaks down long-chain hydrocarbon molecules into shorter, more useful ones, producing alkanes and alkenes.
Cracking using very high temperatures (~1000°C) and high pressure, without a catalyst.
Cracking using a zeolite catalyst at a lower temperature (~500°C) — more energy-efficient than thermal cracking.
An aluminosilicate mineral used as a catalyst in catalytic cracking.

keyPoints.memTip: Cracking = snapping a long spaghetti stick into shorter pieces. More useful, same atoms, just rearranged.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students must know that cracking produces BOTH alkanes AND alkenes. They often forget alkenes — but these are crucial for making polymers.

tables.title: Crude oil fractions — properties and uses

1.question: Why do shorter-chain hydrocarbons rise higher in a fractionating column before condensing?
options: They have lower boiling points, so they stay as vapour at lower temperatures and rise higher
They have higher boiling points, so they need more heat to condense
They are denser and float upwards through the column
They react with the column to gain extra energy
1.correctFeedback: Correct. Shorter chains have lower boiling points — they only condense at the cooler, higher parts of the column.
1.wrongFeedback: Think about what 'lower boiling point' means: the substance becomes a liquid at a lower temperature. In the column, the top is cooler. Shorter chains have lower boiling points so they rise higher before the temperature drops enough for them to condense.

3.question: What are the two types of products always formed when a long-chain hydrocarbon is cracked?
3.correctFeedback: Exactly right. Cracking always gives shorter alkanes (useful as fuels) and alkenes (identified by their C=C double bond, used to make polymers).
3.wrongFeedback: Cracking breaks a long-chain alkane into shorter molecules — always producing shorter alkanes AND alkenes (which have a C=C double bond). No combustion products are formed.

commonMistakes: Describing fractional distillation as a chemical process — it is a physical separation that does not break or form chemical bonds.
Saying longer chains are more flammable — longer chains are LESS flammable (and more viscous) than shorter ones.
Confusing thermal and catalytic cracking — thermal uses very high temperature (~1000°C) and high pressure; catalytic uses a zeolite catalyst at ~500°C.
Forgetting that cracking produces alkenes as well as alkanes — both products are needed for the marks.
Saying crude oil is made of 'molecules of oil' — crude oil is a mixture of hydrocarbons (mostly alkanes), not a single compound.
examTips: The fractionating column trend is always the same: shorter chain = lower BP, more flammable, less viscous, collected higher up.
For cracking questions, state the type (thermal or catalytic), the conditions, and both products (shorter alkane + alkene).
Explain WHY cracking is needed: the demand for short-chain fractions exceeds what natural fractional distillation provides.
revisionCardBullets: Crude oil = mixture of hydrocarbons (mainly alkanes); fossil fuel; non-renewable
Fractional distillation: physical separation by boiling point in a fractionating column
Column: hotter at bottom, cooler at top; shorter chains condense at top
Shorter chain = lower BP, less viscous, more flammable
Refinery gases (C1-C4): LPG; Petrol (C5-C10): cars; Kerosene (C10-C16): jets
Cracking: breaks long-chain alkanes into shorter alkanes + alkenes
Thermal cracking: ~1000°C, high pressure, produces many alkenes
Catalytic cracking: zeolite catalyst, ~500°C, more energy efficient
Alkenes have C=C double bond and are used to make polymers
Cracking needed because demand for petrol/kerosene exceeds natural supply
Example cracking equation: C₁₀H₂₂ → C₈H₁₈ + C₂H₄
